div.head: 282. Letter From President Carter to Governor General of the Solomon Islands DevesiSource: Carter Library, Plains File, President’s Personal Foreign Affairs File, Box 3, Solomon Islands, 6–10/78. No classification marking. The Solomon Islands gained independence on July 7.
div.p: The American people join me in sending best wishes to you and the people of the Solomon Islands. I am pleased to inform you that, as the Solomon Islands attains independence, the United States Government extends its recognition. It is our hope that, with your agreement, diplomatic relations can be established quickly between our two countries.

We believe that friendly and productive relations will develop between the United States and the independent Solomon Islands. Significant ties between our two countries began during World War II when many Americans served in the Pacific area and cooperated closely with Solomon Islanders. These ties have continued, nurtured by our mutual concern for economic progress, human rights, and democratic government.

We are confident that the friendship between our two countries will grow even closer in the years to come. The Government and people of the United States share your aspirations for the progress and welfare of the people of the Solomon Islands, and we look forward to working
together with you in your efforts to realize those goals and to contribute to peace and prosperity for all mankind.
